title: Fear
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Fear
summary: Scripture's repeated command 'do not fear' is not a dismissal of danger but an invitation to ground courage in God's presence and promises rather than in favorable circumstances.
body:
'Do not fear' appears in Scripture more than any other command — not because fear is sinful but because it is universal. God knows the human heart defaults to dread.

When God calls Joshua to lead Israel into hostile territory, the charge is blunt: be strong and courageous, for the Lord your God is with you wherever you go [verse:josh.1.9]. The basis for courage is not Joshua's military skill but God's accompanying presence.

Isaiah delivers the same message to exiles facing an uncertain future: fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name, you are mine [verse:isa.43.1]. The antidote to fear is not the absence of threat but the assurance of belonging.

Jesus addresses fear repeatedly. He calms a storm while his disciples panic, then asks why they are afraid [verse:mark.4.39-40]. He tells a synagogue ruler whose daughter has just died not to fear but only to believe [verse:mark.5.36]. In each case, fear is met not with argument but with the person of Christ.

Paul's mature reflection is that God has not given us a spirit of fear but of power, love, and self-discipline [verse:2tim.1.7]. Fear may visit, but it is not the spirit that defines God's people.

The pattern across Scripture is consistent: fear is acknowledged, not shamed, and countered with presence, not platitudes.

verses.annotation: God's charge to Joshua: courage rooted in divine presence, not personal strength.
God addresses exiled Israel's fear with identity: I have called you by name.
Jesus calms the storm and asks the disciples why they were afraid — the rebuke is for faithlessness, not for the feeling.
Jesus tells Jairus to replace fear with belief at the moment of his daughter's death.
Paul's declaration that fear is not the spirit God has given — power, love, and discipline are.
David's declaration: the Lord is my light and salvation — whom shall I fear?
God's direct assurance: do not fear, I am with you; do not be dismayed, I am your God.

scholars.quote: Oswalt notes that Isaiah's 'fear not' oracles are always grounded in something God has already done — redemption, calling, presence — making courage a response to reality rather than an act of willpower.
scholars.source: The Book of Isaiah, Chapters 40-66 (Oswalt, NICOT, 1998)

scholars.quote: MacArthur reads 2 Timothy 1:7 as Paul's reminder that timidity is incompatible with the Holy Spirit's work — the believer's default posture should be confidence rooted in God's empowerment.

Keener explains that the disciples' fear in the storm narrative would have resonated with ancient audiences for whom the sea symbolized chaos — Jesus' authority over the waves signaled authority over every threat.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)
